info.text: As I explained in the introduction to JavaScript lesson, the DOM (Document Object Model) API, turns our document (our HTML page) into a JavaScript object which we can use to modify it problematically.
info.title: getting started
nn.min.js
info.text: In that lesson I showed you how to interact with this API directly, using the document object (which I also cover in the Date object demo) as well as how to use the netnet standard library to make working with this API a whole lot easier. I've imported the library here (available as nn), without it, this demo would require a lot more code.

info.title: global vars
info.text: In this sketch, fg and bg are declared at the top-level as "global variables" so they’re available across functions. This way they're accessible for both the initial page styling in setup and the butterflies created later in createGif. Making them global keeps things simple by avoiding having to pass them around as parameters.

info.title: color setup
info.text: The setup function chooses two random colors with nn.randomColor(), one for text fg and one for the page background bg. It then checks contrast using nn.colorContrast(fg, bg) and loops until the pair meets a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5. That threshold corresponds to WCAG AA for normal text, so the text stays legible no matter which colors we picked.

info.title: body
info.text: After selecting colors, setup styles the page by grabbing the body via nn.get('body'), setting its content to “Hi World Wide Web!”, and applying layout styles. The foreground and background colors are applied, the viewport is locked with height: '100vh' and overflow: 'hidden', and a simple CSS Grid layout centers the message both horizontally and vertically. A large fontSize turns the text into a bold, poster-like headline.

info.title: 100 gifs
info.text: Lastly, nn.times(100, createGif) calls the createGif function one hundred times. This is a convenience helper: rather than writing a loop, we ask the library to execute a "callback" function some number of times. Each call adds another butterfly somewhere around the scene, quickly filling the page.

info.title: createGif vars
info.text: At the start of createGif, we generate a few variables that control placement and color. x and y are random coordinates chosen slightly beyond the edges of the page, so some butterflies appear just offscreen. r is a random rotation angle in degrees, which gives variety to their orientation.

info.title: color object
info.text: We also create c, the HSL values of the foreground color, using nn.toHSL(fg). Converting the CSS color string into an HSL object is handy because it exposes h (hue), s (saturation), and l (lightness) as numbers we can map into effects. Those values let each butterfly visually harmonize with the text color rather than feeling arbitrary (we'll come back to this).

info.title: create gif
info.text: Then we generate our gif by creating a new img element, setting its 'src' to a butterfly image (one of my pre-packaged gifs) and add it directly to the page's body element.

info.title: place gif
info.text: Then we place teh gif by calling .position(x, y) which moves the element to the random coordinates we computed and .rotate(r) spins the image by r degrees.

info.title: hue shift
info.text: The original butterfly has some flashy colors which might clash with the randomly created color scheme. In order to ensure its color matches our headline text, we apply a few CSS filter-style transforms. .hueRotate(60 - c.h) shifts the hue relative to the text’s hue, nudging the butterflies toward a complementary range.

info.title: color match
info.text: The s and l values fall within a range of 0-100, but our methods expect a range from 0-1 so we need to scale them (divide them by 100). .saturate(c.s / 100) scales saturation based on the text’s saturation, and .brightness(c.l / 100) scales brightness from its lightness.

info.title: transition styles
info.text: The .css() method sets a few presentation details. The important bit is the transition, which asks the browser to animate any future style or transform change over 1.5 seconds. We set this up before the hover behavior so that when we scale, fade, and rotate on mouseover, those changes 'ease-out' smoothly instead of snapping. Since we're going to be interacting with them, the cursor: 'grab' and userSelect: 'none' make them feel interactive without accidental selection highlights.

info.title: mouseover
info.text: Using using the .on() method, we define an "async" function to run when we 'mouseover' the element. Inside the function this is the element itself, the butterfly we moused over. We change its .scale(3), .css('opacity', 0), and .rotate(r - 180), and because of the earlier transition, these changes animate over time (1.5 seconds) instead of changing automatically.

info.title: wait then remove
info.text: The function is defined with async so that we can use await nn.sleep(1500). The sleep blocks the code below from running for 1.5 seconds (1500 milliseconds), matching our transition duration. Awaiting it gives the animation time to finish before we call this.remove(). This avoids removing the element mid-transition and waits until the animation (the hover transition) is complete.

info.title: update logic
info.text: The update function resets the composition. It selects all images with nn.getAll('img'), removes them, and then calls setup() again. That re-picks an accessible color pair, re-styles the page, and repopulates the butterflies, essentially generating a new variation of our page.

info.title: on load
info.text: As with many of our other demos, the last couple of lines in our code sets everything going by running our setup function as soon as our page has loaded. We also set our update to run each time we "click" the page.

info.title: try it out
info.text: Try hovering over the butterflies to see the transition in action, then click on the page a few times to render new variations.
